Python automation: Design a production-r: Use the Resource Manager API to list all projects the service account can see, paginate through resources per project using each service's client library, and collect labels including an 'owner' label with a defined compliance policy (e.g. every project must have owner, cost-center, and environment labels). Flag resources missing required labels or violating naming conventions, write results to CSV with project, resource, missing/invalid labels, and severity, and run it as a scheduled job with alerting on newly non-compliant resources rather than a one-off script.
Python automation: How would you automat: Build a self-service tool (CLI or small API) that takes a namespace request (team, quota tier, network isolation level) and applies a templated set of manifests - Namespace with standard labels, ResourceQuota and LimitRange sized to the tier, RoleBindings scoped to the requesting team's group, and default-deny NetworkPolicies with explicit allow rules. Log every creation with who requested it and when to an audit trail, and make the tool idempotent so re-running it reconciles drift rather than erroring on existing resources.
Bash automation: Write the approach for: Use `df`, `free`, `uptime`/`mpstat` for resource checks, `systemctl --failed` to list failed units, and `curl -sf -m 5` for endpoint health, wrapping each check in a function that returns a pass/fail status and appends to a structured (JSON or key=value) output. Run the script via SSH fan-out (Ansible ad-hoc or a simple parallel-ssh loop) across the fleet, aggregate results centrally, and exit non-zero with a clear summary if any host fails a threshold so it plugs into monitoring or CI.
GCP SDK automation: How would you build: Use the IAM API to list service account keys and their lastUsedTime/creation date, flag keys unused beyond a threshold (e.g. 90 days), and generate a report requiring explicit approval (a ticket, Slack approval, or a --confirm flag with owner sign-off) before disabling rather than auto-deleting. Log every action - key ID, account, timestamp, approver, and outcome - to an audit log or BigQuery table, and always disable before delete so there's a recovery window if a key was flagged incorrectly.
Kubernetes automation: Design a script t: Use the Kubernetes Python or Go client to list pods cluster-wide, inspect container_statuses[].state.waiting.reason for CrashLoopBackOff/ImagePullBackOff, status.phase for Pending, and restart_count against a threshold, and group findings by namespace and owning workload (Deployment/StatefulSet). Output a structured summary (table or JSON) with pod name, reason, and age so on-call engineers can triage quickly, and optionally post it to a Slack channel on a schedule as a proactive health check.
Terraform automation: How would you auto: Chain the checks as CI stages: `terraform fmt -check` and `terraform validate` first (fast, fail early), then tflint for style/best-practice issues, tfsec or Checkov for security misconfigurations, `terraform plan` with the output saved as an artifact, and OPA/Sentinel policy checks against the plan JSON before allowing apply. Post a human-readable plan summary as a PR comment so reviewers see exactly what will change without needing local Terraform access.
Log automation: Build an approach to par: Stream or batch-read structured (JSON) logs, extract service name, exception type/message, and timestamp, and aggregate counts grouped by (service, exception signature) over the incident window using a normalized error signature (stripping variable data like IDs/timestamps from the message so similar errors group together). Rank by count and recency, and output a top-N summary with a sample stack trace per group so responders can triage the highest-impact failure first instead of scrolling raw logs.
API automation: How would you implement: Follow the API's pagination token/cursor pattern in a loop until no next-page token is returned, wrap requests in retry logic with exponential backoff and jitter on 429/5xx, and respect Retry-After headers when present rather than guessing a delay. Authenticate via short-lived tokens refreshed automatically before expiry, and make mutating calls idempotent using client-supplied idempotency keys or by checking current state before applying a change, so a retried request after a timeout does not double-apply.
Configuration automation: How would you: Use a YAML library that preserves comments and formatting (like ruamel.yaml in round-trip mode) rather than a naive load/dump that reformats the whole file, and target only the specific keys that need to change via a path-based update rather than rewriting the whole document. Run the change through a dry-run diff across all target repos first, open PRs automatically (e.g. via a bot) rather than pushing directly, and require review before merge so unexpected formatting changes are caught.
Certificate automation: Design a job tha: Scan certificates via the load balancer/Certificate Manager API and by connecting to endpoints directly (openssl s_client) as a cross-check, compute days-until-expiry, and alert owners (from a certificate-to-owner mapping) at multiple thresholds (30/14/3 days). Track already-sent alerts in a state store keyed by certificate + threshold to avoid duplicate notifications, and after a renewal, verify the new certificate is actually being served (not just issued) before marking the alert resolved.
Cost automation: How would you create a: Export billing data to BigQuery via the standard billing export, run a scheduled query comparing each day's spend by project/service/label against a trailing 7 or 30-day baseline, and flag deviations beyond a statistical threshold (e.g. more than 2 standard deviations or a fixed percentage jump). Attribute cost to teams via cost-center labels enforced by policy, and deliver the report via email/Slack with the top anomalies and a link to the underlying BigQuery data for drill-down.
Backup automation: Design a script that: Trigger backups on a schedule, compute and store a checksum of the backup artifact immediately after creation, and periodically (not just on-demand) perform an actual test restore into an isolated environment to verify the backup is genuinely usable, not just present. Enforce retention by deleting backups older than policy after confirming newer backups exist, and report RPO compliance by comparing the timestamp of the most recent successful (and verified) backup against the target RPO for each system.
CI/CD automation: How would you automati: Build the artifact once (container image or package) tagged with an immutable identifier (git SHA), and promote that exact artifact through environments by updating deployment manifests/references rather than rebuilding per stage. Gate production promotion behind an approval step (manual or automated based on staging metrics), keep the previous production artifact reference available for one-command rollback, and record every promotion and approval in an audit trail.
Security automation: Build a workflow th: Run scheduled scans using Security Command Center findings plus targeted API queries: Storage buckets for public IAM bindings, firewall rules for 0.0.0.0/0 on sensitive ports, IAM policies for broad roles like Owner/Editor on service accounts, and resource configs for missing CMEK/encryption settings. Prioritize findings by exploitability and blast radius, auto-remediate only low-risk, well-understood cases (like a clearly accidental public bucket) with an audit log, and route everything else to owners with a defined SLA.
Incident automation: Which incident-resp: Automate detection, alerting, initial triage data-gathering (logs, metrics, recent deploys), and low-risk mitigations with clear rollback like rolling back to the last known-good deployment or scaling out capacity. Keep human control over anything with ambiguous judgment or high blast radius - declaring a major incident, customer communication, failing over a database, or any action whose wrong call could make things worse - since automation optimizes for the common case and incidents are often the uncommon one.
Python testing: How would you test a clo: Mock the cloud client library calls with unittest.mock or a fake client so tests exercise your logic (parsing, decision-making, error handling) without making real API calls, and use a real but isolated sandbox/dev project for integration tests that do need to hit a live API. Always default the script to dry-run mode in tests and CI, and require an explicit environment check (e.g. refusing to run destructive actions unless GCP_PROJECT matches an allowed non-prod pattern) as a safety net against accidental production runs.
Secrets automation: How would you retrie: Fetch secrets at runtime from Secret Manager into environment variables or in-memory, never as CLI arguments (visible in `ps` and shell history) or committed files, and mark CI variables as masked/protected so they're redacted from job logs automatically. For rotation, generate the new secret, update the secret store, roll it out to consumers, verify they've picked it up, then revoke the old version - never revoke before confirming the new one works, and log rotation events without logging the secret value itself.
Scheduled automation: Compare cron, Kube: Plain cron on a VM is simple but has no built-in retry, alerting, or history, and is a single point of failure; Kubernetes CronJobs add retry and history within the cluster but still require you to build alerting; Cloud Scheduler triggering a Cloud Run job is serverless, has built-in retry/alerting, and scales to zero, good for simple periodic tasks; workflow orchestration (Cloud Composer/Airflow, or Cloud Workflows) is the right choice once jobs have dependencies, need conditional branching, or require visibility into multi-step pipeline state.
Automation reliability: How would you ad: Add a --dry-run flag that logs every intended action without executing it, structured JSON logging with a run ID for traceability, and metrics (items processed, succeeded, failed) emitted to your monitoring system. Add checkpointing so a long-running job can resume from where it left off after a crash, a distributed lock so two instances can't run concurrently and conflict, retry logic for transient failures, and an explicit rollback path (or at minimum a recorded before-state) for any destructive action so a bad run can be undone.
Automation ownership: How would you pack: Package it as a versioned CLI or library published to an internal registry with semantic versioning, write documentation covering usage, required permissions, and failure modes, and release through CI with tests gating every version. Monitor usage and failures via centralized logging/metrics so you know when it breaks before users report it, and treat it like a product with an on-call owner, a changelog, and a deprecation policy rather than a script that quietly rots once the original author moves on.
